patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: use lockless read in nr_cached_objects shrinker callback

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: use lockless read in nr_cached_objects shrinker callback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance issue in the btrfs filesystem by replacing a lock-holding read operation with a lockless one, improving scalability under heavy memory reclaim conditions.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Gave a Reviewed-by tag without any specific comments or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 02:23:46PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> From: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>
> 
> Under heavy memcg-driven slab reclaim with many memcgs and CPUs,
> shrink_slab_memcg() invokes the per-superblock count callback once
> per (memcg, NUMA node) tuple. For btrfs that callback reaches
> percpu_counter_sum_positive() on fs_info->evictable_extent_maps,
> which takes the percpu_counter's raw spinlock with IRQs disabled and
> walks every online CPU. With hundreds of memcgs driving reclaim on a
> host with dozens of CPUs, this counter lock becomes a global
> serialization point: profiles show CPU pinned in the spin_lock_irqsave
> acquire under __percpu_counter_sum, with cross-CPU IPIs hitting
> csd_lock_wait_toolong while waiting for spinning vCPUs.
> 
> The shrinker count is advisory -- super_cache_count() already notes
> "counts can change between super_cache_count and super_cache_scan, so
> we really don't need locks here." Use percpu_counter_read_positive(),
> which is lockless. Worst-case skew is bounded by batch * num_online_cpus
> (a few thousand), negligible compared to the millions of extent maps a
> busy filesystem accumulates and well within the noise that the shrinker
> already tolerates.
> 
> Tested-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> Signed-off-by: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>

Reviewed-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Gave a Reviewed-by tag without any specific comments or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/5/30 06:53, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> From: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>
> 
> Under heavy memcg-driven slab reclaim with many memcgs and CPUs,
> shrink_slab_memcg() invokes the per-superblock count callback once
> per (memcg, NUMA node) tuple. For btrfs that callback reaches
> percpu_counter_sum_positive() on fs_info->evictable_extent_maps,
> which takes the percpu_counter's raw spinlock with IRQs disabled and
> walks every online CPU. With hundreds of memcgs driving reclaim on a
> host with dozens of CPUs, this counter lock becomes a global
> serialization point: profiles show CPU pinned in the spin_lock_irqsave
> acquire under __percpu_counter_sum, with cross-CPU IPIs hitting
> csd_lock_wait_toolong while waiting for spinning vCPUs.
> 
> The shrinker count is advisory -- super_cache_count() already notes
> "counts can change between super_cache_count and super_cache_scan, so
> we really don't need locks here." Use percpu_counter_read_positive(),
> which is lockless. Worst-case skew is bounded by batch * num_online_cpus
> (a few thousand), negligible compared to the millions of extent maps a
> busy filesystem accumulates and well within the noise that the shrinker
> already tolerates.
> 
> Tested-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> Signed-off-by: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu

> ---
>   fs/btrfs/super.c | 2 +-
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/super.c b/fs/btrfs/super.c
> index 958dd185c0d6..c946bccf0748 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/super.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/super.c
> @@ -2432,7 +2432,7 @@ static int btrfs_show_devname(struct seq_file *m, struct dentry *root)
>   static long btrfs_nr_cached_objects(struct super_block *sb, struct shrink_control *sc)
>   {
>   	struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info = btrfs_sb(sb);
> -	const s64 nr = percpu_counter_sum_positive(&fs_info->evictable_extent_maps);
> +	const s64 nr = percpu_counter_read_positive(&fs_info->evictable_extent_maps);
>   
>   	trace_btrfs_extent_map_shrinker_count(fs_info, nr);
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

version_history.subject: [PATCH 00/10] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs
[PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] nfsd: fix BUG_ON in nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid on racing delegation revoke
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the NFSv4 server that can cause a BUG_ON when trying to allocate a layout stateid for a delegated file. The issue occurs when a concurrent delegation revoke is triggered, causing the fi_deleg_file pointer to be cleared under the fi_lock lock. To fix this, the patch takes an RCU read lock around the fi_deleg_file read in the SC_TYPE_DELEG path and replaces the BUG_ON with a graceful error return that cleans up any partially initialized layout stateid. This ensures that the NFS server can handle concurrent delegation revokes without crashing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] nfsd: RCU-protect cl_cb_session to fix use-after-free on session teardown
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFS daemon by converting the cl_cb_session pointer to an RCU-protected pointer. The bug occurs when a session is destroyed while rpciod still holds an inflight callback task that dereferences the freed session. The patch uses RCU (Read-Copy Update) to ensure that readers on rpciod do not access freed memory. It also moves assignments of cl_cb_session to after successful creation of a backchannel and clears it on error returns, and switches from kfree() to kfree_rcu() for freeing the session slab.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] nfsd: convert nfsd_net boolean flags to unsigned long flags word
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the boolean flags in nfsd_net to a single unsigned long flags word, using an enum to define the bit positions. This change fixes a race condition in the grace_ended flag by using test_and_set_bit(), which is atomic on all architectures. The remaining flags are converted to use test_bit/set_bit/clear_bit for consistency and to avoid sub-word cmpxchg issues on certain architectures.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] nfsd: dedup nfs4_client_to_reclaim inserts
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd kernel module where the nfs4_client_to_reclaim function unconditionally allocates new memory for each client, leading to incorrect behavior and potential crashes when the system is rebooted. The fix involves looking up existing clients using nfsd4_find_reclaim_client before allocating new memory, and adding a read-write semaphore to serialize access to the reclaim table. This ensures that the correct records are updated and avoids unnecessary memory allocations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] nfsd: gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the NFSv3 ACL implementation where set_posix_acl() was being called unconditionally, leading to incorrect behavior when setting or removing ACLs. The fix involves adding checks for the argp->mask bits before calling set_posix_acl(), ensuring that only the relevant ACLs are modified. This change mirrors the existing logic in nfsd3_proc_getacl().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] NFSD: check truncate permission under inode lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a timing issue in the NFS server where a client can extend a file before the server checks for truncation permission. The patch splits the write-count acquisition from the truncation permission check, ensuring that the correct size is used when checking for permission. This fix closes a TOCTOU window and prevents concurrent appends from extending the file before the server takes inode lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] nfsd: fix partial-write detection in nfsd_direct_write
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_direct_write function where it incorrectly detects short writes and continues writing to the wrong file offset. The issue arises from the fact that vfs_iocb_iter_write advances the iter by the number of bytes written, making segments[i].iter.count hold the residual length instead of the original request length. To fix this, a snapshot of the segment's byte count is taken before the write and compared against host_err to accurately detect short writes. This ensures that any short write breaks the loop and prevents over-reporting *cnt to the NFS client.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] nfsd: cap decoded POSIX ACL count to bound sort cost
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance issue in the NFS server by capping the number of POSIX ACL entries that can be decoded from a client request. The decoder previously allowed an unlimited number of entries, which led to unbounded CPU usage due to an O(n^2) bubble sort operation. The patch introduces a check to reject requests with more than the maximum allowed number of entries (NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES), preventing excessive CPU usage and ensuring that ACLs set via NFSv4 POSIX ACL extensions are retrievable via NFSACL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] nfsd: validate symlink target length in NFSv4 CREATE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a security vulnerability in the NFSv4 protocol by validating the length of symbolic link targets to prevent a client from forcing an unbounded allocation of memory.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses various bugs and issues in the NFS daemon, including remotely-triggerable panics, use-after-free errors, and denial-of-service vulnerabilities. The patches also include protocol fixes and improvements to the NFSv4 implementation. Specifically, the series includes changes to fix a double-put bug in fuse_try_move_page(), validate symlink target lengths, cap decoded POSIX ACL counts, and gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

version_history.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for fetching NFS server statistics using Generic Netlink, a new feature in the kernel. When netlink is available, nfsstat uses it to retrieve statistics from the server. If netlink is not available (e.g., on older kernels), it falls back to parsing /proc/net/rpc/nfsd as before. The patch adds code to send a dump command and parse the reply to populate various stat arrays, including reply cache, filehandle, IO, network, RPC, and per-version procedure call counts.

review_comments.author: Steve Dickson
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the NFSD_A_SERVER_S enum is not defined anywhere in the patch, and requested its definition to be added

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] support: mark unused arguments in netlink handlers with UNUSED()

version_history.subject: [PATCH] support: mark unused arguments in netlink handlers with UNUSED()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch marks unused arguments in netlink handlers with the UNUSED() macro to suppress -Wunused-parameter warnings.

review_comments.author: Steve Dickson
review_comments.summary: Identified the patch as a good fix for suppressing warnings and committed it to the nfs-utils-2-9-2-rc4 branch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/25/26 8:34 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Use the project's UNUSED() macro from nfslib.h to suppress
> -Wunused-parameter warnings on the error_handler, finish_handler,
> and ack_handler callback parameters that are required by the
> libnl callback signatures but not referenced in the function bodies.
> 
> Fixes: 3ddc8e81240f ("exportfs: release NFSv4 state when last client is unexported")
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
Committed... (tag: nfs-utils-2-9-2-rc4)

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/2] SUNRPC: Check svc pool percpu counter allocation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch introduces a check for svc pool percpu counter allocation, but reviewer Jeff Layton suggests avoiding the hassle of struct randomization and keeping it as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns that struct randomization could break the patch and suggested keeping it as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls to avoid hassle.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Agreed with Jeff that the patch is 'too clever by half', implying disapproval of its approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/30/26 5:48 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Sat, 2026-05-30 at 17:45 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
>> On Sat, 2026-05-30 at 16:21 -0400, Chuck Lever wrote:
>>> From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
>>>
>>> __svc_create() initializes three per-pool percpu_counter stats and
>>> ignores every return value. On SMP, percpu_counter_init() fails when
>>> __alloc_percpu_gfp() cannot satisfy the allocation, leaving the failed
>>> counter with fbc->counters == NULL and its embedded raw_spinlock_t,
>>> list_head, and count never initialized. __svc_create() returns the
>>> half-constructed svc_serv to nfsd, lockd, or the NFS callback service
>>> anyway.
>>>
>>> Once that service is live, the hot-path increments in
>>> svc_xprt_enqueue(), svc_handle_xprt(), and
>>> svc_pool_wake_idle_thread() reach a counter whose backing pointer is
>>> NULL. The pointer is a per-cpu offset, so the access does not fault:
>>> it resolves to offset zero of the current CPU's per-cpu area and
>>> silently corrupts whatever variable lives there. A
>>> /proc/fs/nfsd/pool_stats read walks the same NULL per-cpu storage and
>>> returns garbage, and on CONFIG_DEBUG_SPINLOCK or lockdep it splats on
>>> the never-initialized lock.
>>>
>>> Creating the broken service requires a percpu allocation failure during
>>> RPC server startup, so it is reachable only by a local administrator
>>> under memory pressure or fault injection; a remote peer cannot induce
>>> the bad state on its own.
>>>
>>> Initialize the three adjacent pool counters with one checked
>>> percpu_counter_init_many() call and fail __svc_create() when the
>>> allocation fails, unwinding the counters for pools already set up. Use
>>> the matching percpu_counter_destroy_many() at teardown so the single
>>> per-cpu allocation is freed exactly once.
>>>
>>> Fixes: ccf08bed6e7a ("SUNRPC: Replace pool stats with per-CPU variables")
>>> Signed-off-by: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
>>> ---
>>>  net/sunrpc/svc.c | 32 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++------
>>>  1 file changed, 26 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)
>>>
>>> diff --git a/net/sunrpc/svc.c b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> index ae9ec4bf34f7..aeb6e631848c 100644
>>> --- a/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> +++ b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> @@ -476,6 +476,22 @@ __svc_init_bc(struct svc_serv *serv)
>>>  }
>>>  #endif
>>>  
>>> +enum {
>>> +	SVC_POOL_COUNTERS = 3,
>>> +};
>>> +
>>> +static int svc_pool_init_counters(struct svc_pool *pool)
>>> +{
>>> +	return percpu_counter_init_many(&pool->sp_messages_arrived, 0,
>>> +					GFP_KERNEL, SVC_POOL_COUNTERS);
>>> +}
>>>
>>>
>>
>> Switching to this looks like a good thing, but it means that the
>> svc_pool struct fields now have some strict ordering requirements. The
>> percpu_counters all need to be snuggled up together.
>>
>> That deserves a comment to that effect in the struct svc_pool, so that
>> we don't inadvertently break it later.
>>
> 
> Actually, sashiko points out that struct randomization could break here
> too. Given that service creation isn't a hot codepath, I'd keep these
> as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls and avoid the hassle.
Yeah. It's too clever by half.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: NFSv4.1 COMMIT of all changed areas only on flush? Re: [PATCH 06/10] NFSD: Enable return of an updated stable_how to NFS clients

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/2] sunrpc: init gssp_lock before publishing proc entry
Re: [PATCH 2/2] SUNRPC: Check svc pool percpu counter allocation
patches_acked.patch_summary: This patch adds a comment to the svc_pool struct in the SUNRPC code to note that its percpu counter fields must be ordered together, ensuring future modifications do not inadvertently break this requirement.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged reviewer's concern about potential issues with struct randomization breaking the ordering requirements of svc_pool fields, decided to keep the current implementation as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls to avoid added complexity.
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually, sashiko points out that struct randomization could break here
too. Given that service creation isn't a hot codepath, I'd keep these
as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls and avoid the hassle.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] fuse: disable default bdi strictlimiting
